coredata.dc:title: Non-synthetic luminescent graphene quantum dots in coconut water for aniline sensing applications
coredata.dc:creator: Aly, A.
Ghali, M.
Osman, A.
El Nimr, M.K.
coredata.dc:description: We report, for the first time, on non-synthetic luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) which are found to naturally exist in coconut water. Identification of these GQDs was made using HRTEM, XRD, FTIR and XPS measurements. It was found that such naturally occurring GQDs have dual sizes with average size values of 4.5 nm and 6.1 nm and show luminescence in the UV and blue light regions, respectively. The obtained GQDs also show a unique excitation - independent emission and have a luminescence quantum yield of 0.6 %. The dots were utilized interestingly as an efficient luminescence turn-on optical sensor for aniline liquid detection with a low detection limit 0.234 µM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Coconut water
Aniline sensing

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support for this study by a grant from the Science and Technology Development Fund (STDF) project no. 38311 and project no. 46176. The authors also offer gratitude to the Mitsubishi Co. for supporting this study.

The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support for this study by a grant from the Science and Technology Development Fund (STDF) project no. 38311 and project no. 46176 . The authors also offer gratitude to the Mitsubishi Co. for supporting this study.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM images and the obtained GQDs size distribution of: (a,c) 6.1 nm ± 1.8 nm (b,d) 4.53 nm ± 1.04 nm. (e) XRD measurements of the GQDs showing a broad peak centered around 2θ=26.52◦.
(a) Tapping-mode AFM with topographic height (inset). (b) 13 C NMR spectrum. (c) Raman spectrum.
a) FTIR spectroscopy of the obtained GQDs. (b) XPS measurements of GQDs and high-resolution XPS spectra of (c) C1s and (d) O1s of GQDs.

(a) UV-ViS data of GQDs, aniline and aniline/GQDs combination. (b) PL spectrum of the obtained GQDs under λex = 260 nm where two main PL peaks at 340 nm and 450 nm are observed. The top-right inset is the PL spectrum of the GQDs at different excitation wavelengths. The bottom-left inset shows the same PL spectrum with the low-intensity peaks zoomed on for clarity. (c) Zeta potential measurements of the GQDs. (d) PL lifetime decay measurement GQDs at λP L = 340 nm.
(a) Fluorescence spectrum of GQDs in the presence of different concentrations of aniline (0–4.9430 µM). (b) Calibration curve for aniline for concentrations from 0 to 4.9430 µM.

Histogram data shows the selectivity of GQDs for aniline. The columns represent (I/I0- 1) of the obtained GQDs in the presence of several other: (a) organic solvents and (b) inorganic metals usually present as cationic and ionic in drinking water.
Calibration parameters of the GQD aniline sensor.

head.ce:title: Non-synthetic luminescent graphene quantum dots in coconut water for aniline sensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Basic and Applied Sciences Institute, Egypt-Japan University of Science and Technology, Alexandria 21934, Egypt

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Egypt-Japan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aBasic and Applied Sciences Institute, Egypt-Japan University of Science and Technology, Alexandria 21934, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Kafrelsheikh University, Kafrelsheikh 33516, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bPhysics Department, Faculty of Science, Kafrelsheikh University, Kafrelsheikh 33516, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Physics Department, Faculty of Science, Tanta University, Tanta 31527, Egypt

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Basic and Applied Sciences Institute, Egypt-Japan University of Science and Technology, Alexandria 21934, Egypt.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Egypt-Japan University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report, for the first time, on non-synthetic luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) which are found to naturally exist in coconut water. Identification of these GQDs was made using HRTEM, XRD, FTIR and XPS measurements. It was found that such naturally occurring GQDs have dual sizes with average size values of 4.5 nm and 6.1 nm and show luminescence in the UV and blue light regions, respectively. The obtained GQDs also show a unique excitation - independent emission and have a luminescence quantum yield of 0.6 %. The dots were utilized interestingly as an efficient luminescence turn-on optical sensor for aniline liquid detection with a low detection limit 0.234 µM.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biomass as a renewable source has recently demonstrated its effectiveness as an environmentally friendly source of nanomaterial precursors with low environmentally-adverse effects, low cost, and high biocompatibility [1]. Among various materials explored using biomass and biomass waste, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been investigated and were found to possess many of the qualities of biomass-nanomaterial such as low toxicity, low cost and high water solubility [2]. Multiple synthesis methods of CQDs and GQDs have been studied such as microwave-assisted synthesis [3] and hydrothermal method [4]. The latter is normally used to prepare biomass GQDs at elevated temperatures [5] and the produced dots are usually synthesized from a variety of biomass precursors such as pineapple leaf fiber [6] and brewer's spent grains [7] for a variety of applications including luminescence [8], catalysis [9] and metal ion detection [6].

Coconut water (Cocos nucifera L.) is a natural beverage extracted from the inside of a coconut fruit as a clear fluid and can be drunk as a part of a healthy diet. It has many nutritional values as it is high in potassium and calcium yet low in fats. Coconut water is comprised of over 95 % water content and the rest is a variety of minerals and sugar content [10]. It was demonstrated recently that coconut water can be utilized as a renewable source of carbon nanomaterials, for example, Monoharan et al. synthesized CQDs from coconut water using the acid-assisted ultrasonic method [11]. Also, Preethi et al. [12] prepared coconut water carbon dots using the stirrer-assisted method, where the as-prepared dots showed blue luminescence and were used for optical sensing of Fe3+. Furthermore, Jayan et al. [4] investigated the effects of pressure-free hydrothermal synthesis on tender coconut water by heating the coconut water at elevated temperature until a yellowish-brown solution manifests, indicating precipitation of CQDs. Rahul et al. [13] synthesized CQDs from coconut water through a microwave-assisted method at elevated temperatures (130 - 180 °C) where they observed a tunable luminescence of the dots from blue to green as the synthesis temperature increased. Yet, these synthesis methods suffer from being energy intensive which can be a hurdle to mass production. Meanwhile, Mandani et al. [14] reported earlier on the natural occurrence of luminescent carbon dots in Honey where carbon dots with an average particle size of 3.2 nm ± 1.5 nm were found. They ascribe the source of luminescence to be likely due to a mixture of fluorophores such as polyphenols, vitamins, and amino acids. Also, Dong et al. [15] reported on the natural occurrence of graphene oxide nano-sheets in humic substances.

Moreover, luminescence sensors based on GQDs have been recently used for sensing different analytes like phosphate, Ag+, Fe3+, and Hg2+ based on the PL quenching mechanism [16,17]. On the other hand, aniline is an organic compound with the formula C6H5NH2. It is used in many different industries for materials such as dyes, anti-oxidants, and as an intermediate product [18]. As such, it is a common contaminant that can be found in a lot of mediums (such as water). Exposure to aniline through inhalation, ingestion or skin contact can cause methemoglobinemia which can lead to a variety of medical complications [19]. Thus, accurate detection of aniline is quite important. Different electrochemical and instrument-based methods are frequently used for aniline liquid detection. However, they are expensive and require complex procedures. Recently, carbon dots synthesized at elevated temperature from folic acid (FA) precursor were utilized as PL quenching turn-off sensor for aniline liquid detection [20]. Thus, a low temperature, more simple, and cost-effective method is still needed for monitoring low concentrations of aniline in the environment.

Here, we report for the first time on the production of non-synthetic luminescent GQDs where the dots are found to naturally exist in coconut water at room temperature without any synthesis processes. The extracted GQDs exhibited pronounced optical properties and interestingly utilized in a highly selective and sensitive optical detection of toxic aniline liquid.

To obtain the GQDs, we bought coconut from a local vendor and extracted the water inside it. The coconut water was then filtered by centrifuging and then through 0.22 µm filter paper to remove most of the bulk material such as dirt and scraps of coconut mixed with the water solution. Afterwards, the solution was dialyzed for 24 h to remove carbohydrates and free compounds that may cause luminescence quenching. In white light, the solution shows a murky white color, upon excitation with a UV lamp (λex = 330 nm) the solution reveals a blue emission.

The sensitivity test was done through the standard addition method. Various concentrations (0–4.9 µM) of aniline were added to the GQDs and its fluorescence intensity was measured using a fluorescence spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 260 nm.

The GQDs’ selectivity for aniline was determined by running a selectivity test on a variety of metals (Ni2+, Ca2+, Na+, K+, Fe3+) and Organic solvents (Chloroform, Acetone, Toluene, Ethyl Ether, DMSO, DMF) through mixing 0.4 µM of aniline with 20 µM of analyte.

Fig. 2 shows nanostructures with an average size of 80 nm and average topographic height of 6.67 nm which corresponds to 7 ± 1 of graphene layers [24,25]. The nanoclusters are thought to be made of more than a few GQDs, as shown in TEM Fig. 1. This AFM result along with the shape, and interplanar distance observed from the TEM images as well as the (002) plane from the XRD data confirm that our nanoparticles are graphene quantum dots.

13C NMR measurements were performed to confirm the existence of sp2 carbon that is characteristic of graphene quantum dots (Fig. 2b). 13C spectrum of GQD in DMSO provided evidence to the existence of an sp2 graphitic carbon network. The peak at 87 ppm can be attributed to sp3 defects in the sp2 network. The peaks from 100 to 140 ppm are ascribed to sp2 carbon, within that range, the peak between 120–140 ppm are likely ascribed to aromatic carbon with the peak at 137.5 ppm ascribed to CC groups [26].
Fig. 2(c) shows the Raman spectrum of the GQDs. The characteristic G peak of graphene at 1579 cm−1 as well as another peak related to defects at 1211 cm−1.

FTIR measurements of the obtained GQDs (Fig. 3 a) show seven peaks, the first broad band centered at 3451 cm−1 represents the OH stretching bond of alcohol. The peaks at 2964 and 2922 cm−1 are attributed to CH stretching vibrations. The peaks at 2063 and 1640 cm−1 represent CC and CC stretching bands, respectively. Otherwise, the peaks at 1384 and 1080 cm−1 may be attributed to the stretching bands of COH and CO-C, respectively.

XPS spectra of the obtained GQDs (Fig. 3b) shows two very sharp peaks at 532.5 eV and 286.1 eV corresponding to the C1s and O1s of GQDs, respectively. Trace amounts of N1s can also be found. The C/O ratio was calculated from the spectrum to be 64.56/27.6 suggesting the GQDs have significant oxygen-containing function groups on their surface. The C1s spectra (Fig. 3c) can be deconvoluted into 3 peaks. The first peak at 284.45 eV represents CC bonds, also COH and CO were found at 285.83 eV and 287.3 eV, respectively. Analysis of the O1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) shows two distinct peaks at 530.32 eV and 531.97 eV representing the CO and COH bonds, respectively. This is in good agreement with what we expected given the C/O ratio and is attributed to the oxygen-rich groups functionalized on the surface.

Fig. 4 a) shows the optical absorbance spectrum of the GQDs aqueous solution. A clear absorbance spectral peak with a well-defined shape is observed at 280 nm, corresponding to the π-π∗transition of the CC bonds, consistent with the literature on graphene systems [27]. As for the shoulder after 280 nm peak, this is consistent with n - π∗ transitions of the CO or COOH carboxyl group [28]. Fig. 4a) also shows a 280 nm and 229 nm peaks for aniline which are ascribed to π − π∗ conversion. The solution of GQDs and aniline shows spectroscopic characteristics of both aniline and GQDs which implies the successful combination of both species [20]. Meanwhile, Fig. 4b) shows the PL spectrum of the GQDs with a clear dual peak in the UV and the blue light regions at 340 nm and 450 nm, respectively. The dual peaks observed in the PL spectra suggest the existence of dual-size distributed GQDs in the solution, which agrees with the HRTEM data where two different dot sizes at 6.1 nm ± 1.8 nm and 4.53 nm ± 1.045 nm are found. We distinguish between the dual-sized graphene quantum dots by their size-dependent PL peak position. For the purpose of the optical application, we consider the peak at λ = 340 nm associated with the 4.53 nm dots to be our main peak.

Moreover, as observed in Fig. 4b) top-right and bottom-left inset, the two peaks in the PL spectra reveal no significant change in their peak energy position with the excitation wavelength, which reflects an excitation-independence luminescence behavior in the excitation wavelength range 220 nm–420 nm. However, it is noticed that the GQDs PL intensity varies strongly with the excitation wavelength where the PL intensity reaches its maximum at λex= 280 nm and then diminishes. The later suggests that the origin of our GQDs luminescence is likely to be attributed to the π − π∗ transition of the CC bonds, which is consistent with the absorbance measurements. The observed PL excitation independence behavior suggests also that our non-synthetic GQDs, from the coconut water, have less radiative trap centers and/or defect states [29]. It should be noted that such GQDs excitation-independent emission was also observed recently in e.g., biomass-synthesized GQDs prepared at high microwave power irradiation (900 W for 180 min) and by micro-plasma assisted electrochemical synthesis [30]. Compared to these works, our GQDs are remarkably energy, time, and cost-effective since no synthesis procedure is applied and the process of filtration is made at room temperature. The excitation-independent emission behavior adds value to our GQDs as it enables these natural dots to have applications in different fields e.g., quantum encryption [31]. Moreover, in order to explore the GQDs’ surface charge, Zeta potential measurements were conducted on an aqueous solution of the dots. The results are described in Fig. 4c). As seen the dots show a Zeta potential of −23.8 eV which reflects that the obtained GQDs’ surface has a negative charge with a tendency to be highly electro-stable in water.

As the PL intensity of the GQDs with an average size of 4.51 nm ± 1.04 nm is remarkably higher than that of the dots with an average size of 6.1 nm ± 1.8 nm, we give consideration of these smaller size dots. For example, the high PL intensity of the dots with an average size of 4.51 nm enabled us to measure their excitonic lifetime data (Fig. 4d) by time-resolved PL technique, and the best fit of the PL decay was made with the second-order exponential decay function.
(1)
y = A 1 × e x p
( − x /
t 1
)
+ A 2 × e x p
( − x /
t 2
)
+
y 0
Where A is the amplitude of the lifetime component and t is the respective lifetime value.

The average lifetime was calculated to be 5.3 ns which is a relatively long time suitable for optical application. In the following, we will utilize these dots for application as an optical probe of aniline liquid.

The quantum yield was calculated through the equation:
(2)
Q S
=
Q R
×
(
m S
/
m R
)
×
(
n S
/
n R
)
2
Where Q is the quantum yield, m is the slope of the curve of integrated fluorescence vs. absorbance and n is the refractive index. The subscripts S and R refer to the GQDs and the reference Tryptophan, respectively. The quantum yield of tryptophan is 14 %. The GQDs were dissolved in water and their concentration was adjusted to get an absorbance value <0.1 to avoid reabsorption effects. It was found that the GQDS PL QY is 0.6 %.

Aniline with different concentrations in the range between 0 µM and 4.9 µM has been prepared and added to a 200 µg/ml concentration of an aqueous solution of the obtained GQDs for aniline sensing. Fig. 5 a) shows the PL spectra of the GQDs where an enhancement of the PL intensity with aniline is clearly seen. Fig. 5b) summarizes the linear changes in the fluorescence integrated intensities I/I0 with aniline concentration for the dots with an average size of 4.51 nm, where I and I0 are the fluorescence intensities of the GQDs in the presence and absence of aniline, respectively. The results show that GQDs can act as an excellent turn-on optical sensor to aniline (Fig. 5a) as demonstrated by an R2 value of 0.99 (Fig. 5b). From the linear relation between I/I0 and aniline concentration, the limit of detection (LOD) was evaluated through the relation
(3)
L O D = 3 σ ÷ S
Where σ is the standard deviation of the response and S is the calibration curve's slope. The LOD was calculated to be 0.234 µM. The calibration data and the GQDs sensor parameters are summarized in Table 1 . This value is lower than most obtained by other methods for aniline liquid detection [32,33] and to our knowledge, is the first report in literature of aniline sensing by GQDs which may introduce yet another new application for the material. It should be noted that the intercept value was found to be 0.996, which is very close to 1, which demonstrates that the fluorescence effects can be mainly attributed to the aniline component and not to any other interference sources like water [20]. The increased GQDs luminescence by aniline is ascribed to the GQDs's surface passivation caused by the electron transfer interaction between the -NH2 amine group in the aniline and the surface states on the GQDs [34]; which is facilitated by the fact that our surface is rich in oxygen compounds as demonstrated by the XPS and the FTIR measurements. This process occurs through partial substitution of -OH with -NH. This interaction is facilitated by the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the aniline molecules and the linkers on the GQDs surface (mainly oxygen function groups). The FRET phenomenon occurs when there is an overlap between the donor's emission spectrum and the acceptor's absorption spectrum [35,36]. It is well known that aniline has an emission band at an excitation wavelength of 260 nm and a maximum emission when at an excitation wavelength of 284 nm. This is the same range in which the GQDs function as a linear sensor which causes the FRET process to trigger and for the aniline molecules to function as a donor. This also explains why our GQDs’ luminescence is enhanced by aniline rather than quenched by it as has been reported in other carbon nanomaterials whose emission and excitation bands are higher than ours and misaligned with that of aniline [20,37].

The selectivity of aniline among other interference sources has been investigated in detail. As mentioned before, The FRET process along with the nitrogen functionalization is what gives aniline its unique turn-on behavior with the GQDs, it is also what gives aniline its unique selectivity turn-on behavior with the GQDs different from other measured analytes. This unique behavior is due to either having excitation bands misalignment with our GQDs or being unable to functionalize the surface readily with nitrogen compounds that are widely reported to enhance the optoelectronic features of GQDs [38,39]. In this test, a 200 µg/ml aqueous solution of GQD with aniline concentration of 0.4 µM was measured against 20 µM of and organic solvents to evaluate its selectivity for aniline. Namely, we studied the selectivity of aniline among other solvents including chloroform, Acetone, Toluene, EE, DMSO, and DMF (Fig. 6 a). As shown in Fig. 6, we find that aniline presents stronger GQDs luminescence enhancement (turn-on) than that of other investigated chemicals which, conversely, reveal a strong GQDs luminescence quenching (turn-off). This shows the unique behavior of aniline with our GQDs which is not replicated in any of the other measured analytes. Thus, making the GQDs a highly selective sensor for aniline detection among organic solvents. Moreover, the obtained GQDs sensor was used for the determination of aniline in the presence of 20 µM of each of Ni2+, Ca2+, Na+, K+, Fe3+ ions without interferences from these species (Fig. 6b). These ions are the major cations and anions usually found in water samples. Again, and as shown in Fig. 6, the GQDs luminescence enhances with aniline and quenches with other ions, and hence proves that the GQDs are highly sensitive for aniline than the other cations or anions. These data reveal that our GQDs are interference-free towards many organic and inorganic species and thus can be applied uniquely for trace determination of aniline in e.g., water samples.

In conclusion, GQDs were prepared through an energy-conservative, biologically friendly non- synthetic route. The GQDs were shown to have excellent sensitivity and selectivity for aniline with a potential application as an aniline PL turn-on sensor. This interesting property of natural nanomaterial occurrence in coconut water could open up more future research to study the natural formation of nanomaterial in organic substances.
A. Aly: Methodology, Writing – original draft. M. Ghali: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. A. Osman: Writing – original draft. M.K. El Nimr: Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support for this study by a grant from the Science and Technology Development Fund (STDF) project no. 38311 and project no. 46176. The authors also offer gratitude to the Mitsubishi Co. for supporting this study.
